So today we're going to talk about vectorized query execution.

OLAP system is going to get good query performance.

divide it up into pipelines and run them in parallel.

run it as much as I can in about query compilation and co-generation.

and aware clause or a join clause.

or just in compilation and the co-generation stuff we'll talk about on Wednesday this week.
And then we also introduced the idea of query at adaptivity.
We're not going to push in this too much just yet, but it's the idea that the optimizer
spits out a query plan and then it run time while the database system is executing that query,
it can decide whether that query plan was a good idea or not and can make some changes
either to change the ordering that it checks predicates,
what co-path would use to do certain things.
But then we'll see later in the semester how to do bigger things like change the actual query plan
on the fly while we're running.
So today's task or today's class is going to be about vectorization.
And the idea here is that we want to take the scalar algorithms that we discussed in the introduction class
where we're going to operate on a single tuple at a time and in some cases,
even a single operand at a time.
And we're going to convert them into a vectorized form and rely on a simpede instructions
that the CPUs provide for us to be able to run multiple operations within an operator
or expression, whatever we're trying to do, at the same time.
And so this is to be notion as data parallelization.
We're going to have multiple computations occurring at the same time for multiple pieces of data.
And then simpede is going to achieve this.
So why does this matter?
Well, again, the same way that scaling out across multiple threads, processes, or nodes,
it's going to give us additional improvement performance because we're not restricted to what a single thread on a single core can do.
In some cases, we can get even bigger speed up because of SIMD because that also can run parallel across multiple cores.
And then the speed up we'll get will be multiplicative.
So every core we can have a data parallel algorithm and across all those cores, they're all running at the same time.
So let's say that on a machine that has 32 cores, assuming I can scale out perfectly linearly, then I can divide my task up into 32 discrete tasks.
So that's a 32x speed up.
And then if I can have a portion of that computation, ignoring how we get 2ples in and out for now, that can run on using SIMD and can do process 4 2ples at a time.
So then it's 32x times 4x.
So in theory, for this scenario here, we could get up to 128x improvement in performance.
And that's just on a single node. And that's pretty significant.
Anything that's, you know, at least an order magnitude is a huge win.
Two orders magnitude would be unheard of.

Now, we're never going to come close to this because as I was saying, there's a bunch of stuff we have to do to get things in and out of the registers, in and out between operators, copying things from disk, sending things over the network.
Like this, we're never even going to come close.
And in some best cases scenario, when we look at some vectorized algorithms, we might get 1.4x speed up if we're lucky.

So I don't want to spend too much time in it.
And the idea goes back to this notion of this classification of what these instructions are actually going to be.
Goes back to the 1960s.
There was this thing called Flynn's taxonomy where he described what system instructions are.
SIMD, and I think MIMD as well.

But obviously now in the 2020s, you know, these things have been around for quite a while.
So we can exploit them and use them inside of our database systems.
So SIMD is going to be a class of CPU instructions that can allow, again, our processor to do multiple, to do the same operation on multiple pieces of data at the same time.
And the way this is going to work is that we're going to rely on these special SIMD registers as a way to get things into these instructions and out of these instructions.
And the overall goal as we go through is that we want to keep things out in the SIMD registers for as long as possible, do as much processing as we can.
And then tape you guys read talked about because ADX 512, we can achieve this now better than we used to.
You want to keep things out in the SIMD registers as long as possible and only bring it out to the CPU cache or memory when we're done with whatever it is what we're going to do.
So we're going to focus most of this lecture on ADX 512.
But this is showing here that there's every other ISA has their own variants of them.
And in the case of Intel, it goes back to the 1990s when they first put up these MMX stuff.

We want to do a simple operation, take two matrices, X plus Y, add them together,
and produce the new matrix, Z.
So again, if you're going to write this with scalar code or using system instructions,
you just have a for loop that iterates over every element of X and I,
and then write out the Z.
So your literally scaling or going through the each element of the two arrays, one by one,
running one instruction, adding together, and then one store instruction to put it out into the output buffer Z.

But for now, at the end of the day, it's still going to execute a single instruction to add two numbers together,
to write it out to another register, or another write it out to memory.
So SIMD, what we can do is we can take a vector of values,
and assuming here we're doing 32 bit numbers for elements, so it's a 120 bit bit register,
can A, V, X, 5, 12, it's going to be 512 bit registers, so we can put more things in there.
So now it's going to be one SIMD instruction to add up the offsets,
the matching offsets across the two registers, and produce a single output.
And then do the same thing for the other one, add it together and produce the output.
So what took before eight instructions, to do eight addition instructions,
now we can do it down to two.
Right.
This is why this is going to be important, obviously for databases,
if we're trying to rip through columns and columns of billions and tuples,
we want to be able to take advantage of this.
So there's two type of vectorization we can have in our system, in our data system.
The first is what I just showed, or the first would be what is called horizontal vectorization,
where the idea is that you're going to have some instruction that's going to take all the elements within a SIMD register,
and then produce a single scalar output.
Like if I want to get the summation of all the elements within this four-lane register here,
there's some instruction that can do that, and then produces some scalar output there.
Early CPUs don't support this.
It's mostly found in the newer CPUs that can do, or at least on the x86,
that can do AVX2, which is the precursor to the AVX512.
But this is not going to be entirely useful for the stuff we want to do in databases.
The one we care about is vertical vectorization, where the i...
